description: CJC-1295 is a synthetic growth hormone releasing hormone (GHRH) analog engineered for a longer half-life than natural GHRH, commonly combined with Ipamorelin in gray-market protocols; its GH/IGF-1 mechanism has early pharmaceutical-development data behind it, but no FDA approval and an uncertain compounding pathway.

legacy.human_evidence_count: Early pharmaceutical-development human pharmacokinetic data exists for GHRH analogs; CJC-1295-specific long-term human trials are lacking.
legacy.regulatory_changelog: 2023-04/2026: FDA Category 2 restricted (503A bulk substances). 2026-04: Removed from Category 2 following nomination withdrawal (gray zone, not Category 1). Not included on the July 23-24, 2026 PCAC review agenda (which covers BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, MOTS-C, Emideltide/DSIP, Semax, and Epitalon).
legacy.regulatory_federal: Sellable only as research use only (RUO) product; compounding-pharmacy pathway is uncertain and not scheduled for near-term PCAC review.
legacy.runtime_safety: The DAC form's sustained, non-pulsatile GH elevation is a meaningfully different physiological exposure than the body's natural pulsatile pattern; sustained supraphysiological GH/IGF-1 exposure is associated with known risks (insulin resistance, growth-factor-related concerns) in the broader GH-axis literature, though CJC-1295-specific long-term human data does not exist. No completed long-term human safety trials exist for either form. RUO sourcing carries the same purity and mislabeling risks as other gray-market peptides.
legacy.source_tables: Compound Master V3; KG Nodes v5; AI Retrieval Registry; Runtime Visibility Registry; Search Intent Registry; Recommendation Engine Registry

description: Semaglutide is a GLP-1 receptor agonist, FDA-approved as Ozempic, Wegovy, and Rybelsus, backed by a large multi-trial Phase 3 program spanning weight management, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular risk reduction, and kidney outcomes — a categorically stronger evidence tier than the RUO peptides elsewhere in this series.

legacy.human_evidence_count: Large multi-trial Phase 3 program (STEP, SELECT) spanning weight management, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular outcomes.
legacy.regulatory_changelog: 2026: FDA shortage determination resolved for semaglutide, winding down the compounded-supply era; 503B bulk compounding no longer available absent individualized clinical justification.
legacy.regulatory_federal: FDA-approved and commercially available by prescription. The FDA has determined semaglutide is no longer in shortage, curtailing 503B outsourcing facilities' ability to compound it in bulk; compounded access now generally requires individualized physician-documented clinical necessity rather than cost or availability alone. Not sold as a research peptide.
legacy.runtime_safety: Common side effects are nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and constipation, most pronounced during dose escalation. Less common but notable: gallbladder disease, rare pancreatitis, and aspiration risk under anesthesia due to delayed gastric emptying. Carries a boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumor risk observed in rodent studies and is contraindicated in anyone with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2. Not for use in pregnancy.
legacy.source_tables: Compound Master V3; KG Nodes v5; AI Retrieval Registry; Runtime Visibility Registry; Search Intent Registry; Recommendation Engine Registry

data.class_or_domain: Growth hormone secretagogue (ghrelin receptor agonist) peptide
data.dosage: No established consumer dosing protocol; see full guide article for regulatory and safety context.
data.keywords: Ipamorelin; growth hormone secretagogue; peptide therapy
data.legal_status: Not FDA-approved as a finished drug product. Not removed from Category 2 in the same April 2026 action as BPC-157/TB-500/CJC-1295, but named among peptides expected to move toward Category 1 (compoundable) status per the February 2026 HHS/FDA announcement; formal Federal Register rulemaking was still pending as of mid-2026.
data.mechanism_summary: Ghrelin Receptor (GHS-R1a) Agonism; Selective Pulsatile GH Release; Minimal Cortisol/Prolactin Cross-Reactivity
data.primary_effects: Ghrelin Receptor (GHS-R1a) Agonism; Selective Pulsatile GH Release; Minimal Cortisol/Prolactin Cross-Reactivity
data.safety_notes: No major safety signals in short-term human studies, but no long-term data exists. Theoretical concerns tied to GH-axis stimulation support physician-supervised rather than self-directed use.
data.topic_ecosystems: Hormonal Health; Exercise Performance

description: Ipamorelin is a selective ghrelin-receptor agonist (growth hormone secretagogue) with more human clinical data than most peptides in this class, showing reliable dose-dependent GH release with minimal cortisol/prolactin cross-reactivity; downstream body-composition and sleep claims are more extrapolated than proven.

legacy.human_evidence_count: Human studies from 1990s-2000s pharmaceutical development (including postoperative-ileus research) confirm the core GH-release mechanism.
legacy.regulatory_changelog: 2026-02: Named among ~14 of 19 originally Category-2-restricted peptides expected to move back toward Category 1 per HHS/FDA announcement. Mid-2026: Formal Federal Register rulemaking confirming final category not yet published.
legacy.regulatory_federal: No prescription form exists on the market; product is RUO or physician-prescribed compounded (where accessible). Prohibited under WADA's S2 category for competitive athletes.
legacy.runtime_safety: Short-term human studies have not identified major safety signals at studied doses, but long-term (multi-year) safety data does not exist. Because it stimulates endogenous GH release, theoretical concerns around GH's known effects (insulin sensitivity changes, potential growth-factor-driven proliferation effects) apply, supporting physician-supervised use rather than self-directed protocols. RUO-sourced product quality is not independently verified.
legacy.source_tables: Compound Master V3; KG Nodes v5; AI Retrieval Registry; Runtime Visibility Registry; Search Intent Registry; Recommendation Engine Registry

description: Tirzepatide is a dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist, FDA-approved as Mounjaro and Zepbound, showing the largest average weight-loss effect of any approved obesity drug to date (up to ~22.5% body weight at 72 weeks in SURMOUNT-1) and superior HbA1c reduction versus semaglutide in a head-to-head trial.

legacy.human_evidence_count: SURMOUNT and SURPASS-2 Phase 3 trial programs, including a head-to-head superiority result versus semaglutide on HbA1c.
legacy.regulatory_changelog: 2026: FDA shortage determination resolved for tirzepatide, winding down the compounded-supply era; 503B bulk compounding no longer generally available absent individualized clinical justification.
legacy.regulatory_federal: FDA-approved and commercially available by prescription. As with semaglutide, the FDA has determined tirzepatide is no longer in shortage, curtailing 503B bulk compounding; compounded access now generally requires individualized physician-documented clinical necessity. Not sold as a research peptide.
legacy.runtime_safety: Common side effects are nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, and constipation, dose-dependent and most common during titration. Less common but notable: gallbladder disease, rare pancreatitis, injection-site reactions, and aspiration risk under anesthesia. Carries the same boxed warning as semaglutide for thyroid C-cell tumor risk observed in rodent studies, with the same contraindication for personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN 2. Not for use in pregnancy.
legacy.source_tables: Compound Master V3; KG Nodes v5; AI Retrieval Registry; Runtime Visibility Registry; Search Intent Registry; Recommendation Engine Registry

description: BPC-157 is a synthetic 15-amino-acid peptide studied mainly in animal models for gut healing, tendon and ligament repair, and inflammation modulation; human clinical trial data is essentially absent and its FDA compounding status is unresolved pending a July 2026 PCAC review.

legacy.human_evidence_count: Human clinical trial data is essentially absent; evidence is almost entirely preclinical (rodent/in vitro).
legacy.regulatory_changelog: 2023-04/2026: FDA Category 2 restricted (503A bulk substances). 2026-04-22: Removed from Category 2 (gray zone, not Category 1 approved). 2026-07-23/24: FDA PCAC scheduled to review for 503A compounding eligibility, alongside KPV, TB-500, and MOTS-C.
legacy.regulatory_federal: Not FDA-approved as a finished drug product; RUO-labeled product is not legally intended for human consumption. Banned year-round under WADA's S0 category.
legacy.runtime_safety: No large-scale human safety trials exist and long-term human safety is unknown. Animal toxicology has not identified major red flags at studied doses, but this does not establish human safety. RUO-sourced product purity, sterility, and actual peptide content are not independently verified — contamination and mislabeling are documented risks in the gray-market peptide industry. Use only under licensed physician guidance.

description: TB-500 is a synthetic fragment of thymosin beta-4 marketed for muscle recovery and connective-tissue repair; the underlying Tβ4 biology has real scientific grounding, but the synthetic fragment sold as TB-500 has thin direct human evidence and an unresolved FDA compounding status pending July 2026 PCAC review.

legacy.human_evidence_count: Direct human trial data for the synthetic TB-500 fragment is thin; broader Tβ4 biology has more preclinical grounding.
legacy.regulatory_changelog: 2023-04/2026: FDA Category 2 restricted (503A bulk substances), alongside BPC-157 and 17 other peptides. 2026-04: Removed from Category 2 (gray zone). 2026-07-23: On FDA PCAC agenda alongside BPC-157, KPV, and MOTS-C for formal 503A eligibility review.
legacy.regulatory_federal: Not FDA-approved for human use; RUO-labeled product is not legally intended for human consumption.
legacy.runtime_safety: No controlled human safety data exists for the synthetic TB-500 fragment. Because Tβ4 supports angiogenesis and cell migration broadly, some researchers urge caution in anyone with a personal or family history of cancer, though this has not been demonstrated for TB-500 in humans. RUO-sourced product carries the same purity, sterility, and mislabeling risks common across the gray-market peptide industry.
legacy.source_tables: Compound Master V3; KG Nodes v5; AI Retrieval Registry; Runtime Visibility Registry; Search Intent Registry; Recommendation Engine Registry

data.dosage: Research doses are indication-specific. Long-term respiratory trials commonly evaluated oral NAC around 600–1,200 mg/day, while psychiatric adjunct trials often used 2,400–3,000 mg/day; these study protocols should not be treated as universal self-care dosing.
data.mechanism_summary: Inflammatory Signaling Modulation; Oxidative Stress Modulation; Stress Response Modulation; Neuroprotective Activity
data.primary_effects: glutathione system, oxidative stress pathways, glutamate signaling, mucus disulfide bonds, glutathione pathways
data.safety_notes: Oral NAC is generally well tolerated; nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and other gastrointestinal discomfort are the most common adverse effects. Review use with nitroglycerin, antitussives, and some oral antibiotics. Severe anaphylactoid reactions and bronchospasm are primarily associated with parenteral administration, so asthma cautions should remain route-specific.

description: NAC is a cysteine donor used medically as a mucolytic and as an antidote for acetaminophen overdose. For consumer guidance, its strongest relevant evidence is respiratory mucus and exacerbation context plus glutathione/redox biology; psychiatric uses remain preliminary, adjunctive, and diagnosis-specific.

legacy.evidence_risk_of_bias: Respiratory evidence combines different mucolytics, doses, COPD phenotypes, background therapies, and study eras, with substantial heterogeneity. Psychiatric trials are comparatively small, mixed, diagnosis-diverse, and usually adjunctive. Outcome claims must remain indication-specific and should not imply established disease treatment.
legacy.human_evidence_count: Merged decision-layer upgrade: best framed for respiratory mucus support; glutathione replenishment; compulsive-behavior research context; confidence high.

data.legal_status: Bremelanotide is FDA-approved as Vyleesi, prescription-only, for HSDD in premenopausal women specifically. PT-141 sold as a research chemical is a separate regulatory category, not FDA-approved for any use outside that narrow indication, and is not on the Category 1/2 peptide compounding lists that cover BPC-157, TB-500, etc.
data.mechanism_summary: Melanocortin Receptor (MC3R/MC4R) Agonism; Central Nervous System Arousal Pathway Activation
data.primary_effects: Melanocortin Receptor (MC3R/MC4R) Agonism; Central Nervous System Arousal Pathway Activation
data.safety_notes: Can transiently raise blood pressure (monitor with cardiovascular risk factors); nausea is common; repeated use can cause focal skin/mucosal hyperpigmentation. Off-label uses are far less evidenced than the approved HSDD indication.
data.topic_ecosystems: Hormonal Health

description: PT-141 is the research-chemical name for bremelanotide, a melanocortin receptor agonist with an actual FDA-approved product (Vyleesi) for hypoactive sexual desire disorder in premenopausal women; most gray-market marketing (including to men, for erectile dysfunction) extends well beyond that narrow approved indication on much thinner evidence.

legacy.human_evidence_count: Phase 3 trials (Vyleesi/bremelanotide) established efficacy for HSDD in premenopausal women; off-label use cases have thinner trial support.
legacy.regulatory_federal: Vyleesi (bremelanotide) is FDA-approved prescription-only for HSDD in premenopausal women. RUO-labeled PT-141 product is not legally intended for human consumption and is not the same regulatory entity as Vyleesi.
legacy.runtime_safety: Bremelanotide can transiently raise blood pressure and, in a subset of patients, cause a notable spike — Vyleesi carries specific dosing-frequency limits partly for this reason, a meaningful consideration for anyone with cardiovascular risk factors. Nausea is one of the most commonly reported trial side effects. Repeated use can cause focal skin/mucosal hyperpigmentation, a known melanocortin-receptor class effect. Off-label uses (male sexual enhancement, general libido) have not been evaluated to the same standard as the approved HSDD indication.
legacy.source_tables: Compound Master V3; KG Nodes v5; AI Retrieval Registry; Runtime Visibility Registry; Search Intent Registry; Recommendation Engine Registry

data.legal_status: Topical GHK-Cu is legally sold as a cosmetic ingredient (does not require FDA drug approval for appearance-related cosmetic claims). Injectable GHK-Cu, sold as a research peptide, is not FDA-approved as a drug and was named among peptides expected to move toward Category 1 compounding status per the February 2026 HHS/FDA announcement, though formal rulemaking was still pending as of mid-2026.
data.mechanism_summary: Copper Cofactor Delivery (Lysyl Oxidase and Superoxide Dismutase); Collagen and Elastin Synthesis Stimulation; Antioxidant and Anti-Inflammatory Activity; Wound-Healing Angiogenesis Support
data.primary_effects: Copper Cofactor Delivery (Lysyl Oxidase and Superoxide Dismutase); Collagen and Elastin Synthesis Stimulation; Antioxidant and Anti-Inflammatory Activity; Wound-Healing Angiogenesis Support
data.safety_notes: Topical use is well-tolerated with occasional mild irritation. Injectable/systemic use has far less human safety data and should involve physician oversight, particularly for anyone with a copper-metabolism condition such as Wilson's disease.
data.topic_ecosystems: Inflammation; Oxidative Stress

description: GHK-Cu is a naturally occurring copper-binding tripeptide with a genuinely well-supported topical cosmetic evidence base — including human RCTs for skin firmness, fine lines, and wound healing — plus a separate, much less-evidenced injectable/RUO research-peptide use.

legacy.human_evidence_count: Multiple human RCTs of topical formulations report improvements in fine lines, skin firmness, and photodamage versus vehicle/placebo.
legacy.regulatory_changelog: 2026-02: Injectable GHK-Cu named among peptides expected to move toward Category 1 compounding status per HHS/FDA announcement. Mid-2026: Formal rulemaking still pending.
legacy.regulatory_federal: Topical cosmetic use is a legitimate, established commercial channel. Injectable/systemic use falls into the same RUO regulatory category as other research peptides.
legacy.runtime_safety: Topical GHK-Cu has a good safety track record in cosmetic use, with occasional mild irritation reported at higher concentrations. Injectable/systemic GHK-Cu safety data in humans is much more limited than the topical literature, and shares the same RUO sourcing and quality-control caveats as other gray-market peptides. Copper is essential but can be harmful in excess; systemic use should be approached with more caution and ideally physician oversight, particularly for anyone with a condition like Wilson's disease affecting copper metabolism.
legacy.source_tables: Compound Master V3; KG Nodes v5; AI Retrieval Registry; Runtime Visibility Registry; Search Intent Registry; Recommendation Engine Registry

description: Chamomile is a traditional botanical most often used for sleep, relaxation, mild anxiety, and digestive comfort. A small randomized trial points to modest benefit for generalized anxiety, while evidence for sleep and digestion is more limited and lower-certainty, so effects are best framed as gentle rather than dramatic. It is generally well tolerated; people with ragweed-family (Asteraceae) allergies should be cautious, it may add to the effects of sedatives, and pregnancy warrants caution — discuss use with a clinician.
